summary:
The Association of Southeast Asian Nations, founded in August 1967 by Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, and Thailand, grew from a Cold War security arrangement into the world's most successful regional organization outside Europe by the end of the twentieth century. The founding Bangkok Declaration was motivated partly by shared concern about communist expansion and internal insurgencies in the immediate aftermath of the Vietnam War era. Brunei joined in 1984, Vietnam in 1995, Laos and Myanmar in 1997, and Cambodia in 1999, completing the organization's membership across mainland and maritime Southeast Asia.

ASEAN's success rested on the principle of non-interference in members' internal affairs — the ASEAN Way — which allowed a diverse group of states with different political systems, religions, and colonial histories to cooperate without friction over sovereignty. The organization facilitated growing intraregional trade, investment, and infrastructure connectivity during the economic boom years of the 1980s and 1990s, when several ASEAN members — the Tiger economies of Singapore, Malaysia, and Thailand — achieved some of the fastest economic growth rates in the world. The 1997 [[event:2769|Asian Financial Crisis]] exposed vulnerabilities in these economic models but ultimately strengthened the case for deeper regional financial cooperation.
